title: Viewing File Contents
summary: Inspect text files with cat, head, tail, and wc.

sketchBlocks.title: Read a whole file with cat
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
`cat` prints the contents of a text file to the terminal. It is useful when the file is short and you want to see everything at once.

Example:

```bash
cat notes.txt
```

If `notes.txt` contains:

```text
Buy folders
Review chapter 2
Email project partner
```

then `cat notes.txt` shows all three lines in the terminal.

Step by step:
- `cat` is the command.
- `notes.txt` is the file you want to read.
- The terminal prints the file exactly as it is stored.

Use `cat` for quick inspection of small text files. For longer files, `head` and `tail` are often easier to use.

sketchBlocks.title: Preview the beginning or end with head and tail
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes you do not need the whole file. `head` shows the first part of a file, and `tail` shows the last part.

Example:

```bash
head reading-log.txt
tail reading-log.txt
```

If `reading-log.txt` has many lines, `head` helps you check how it starts, while `tail` helps you check the newest or last entries.

For a beginner-friendly way to think about them:
- `head` = top of the file
- `tail` = bottom of the file

Worked example:

```text
Monday
Tuesday
Wednesday
Thursday
Friday
Saturday
Sunday
```

- `head` would show the first lines, starting with `Monday`.
- `tail` would show the last lines, ending with `Sunday`.

These commands are especially helpful when a file is too long to read comfortably with `cat`.

sketchBlocks.title: Count lines, words, and characters with wc
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
`wc` gives counts for a file. It is often used to count lines, words, and characters in text.

Example:

```bash
wc announcement.txt
```

A result might look like this:

```text
3 12 68 announcement.txt
```

That means:
- `3` lines
- `12` words
- `68` characters

You do not need to memorize every number position right away. The main idea is that `wc` summarizes how much text is in the file.

Worked example:

If `announcement.txt` contains three short sentences, `wc announcement.txt` quickly tells you the file is short without printing the whole text.

This is useful when checking note length, comparing files, or confirming that a file has the amount of content you expect.

quizDraft.title: Choose the best command for the last lines
quizDraft.prompt: A club keeps adding new entries to `updates.txt`. Which command is best when you want to quickly see the end of that file?
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.hint_1: Compare each option to the exact topic named in the question.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.title: When cat is a good fit
quizDraft.prompt: A student assistant is checking files in a class notes folder. Which tasks are good uses for `cat`? Choose all that apply.
options: Read every line of a short checklist file
See only the last lines of a long log file
Display the full contents of a short reminder note
Count how many words are in an essay draft
quizDraft.hint: Think about when seeing the entire text at once is helpful.

help.concept: `cat` prints the full contents of a file to the terminal, so it works best for short text files when you want to read everything.
help.hint_1: Look for tasks where the learner wants the whole file, not just the beginning, end, or a count.
help.hint_2: Two choices involve reading all text directly. The others are better matched to `wc`, `head`, or `tail`.

quizDraft.title: What wc tells you
quizDraft.prompt: You run `wc draft.txt` while reviewing a student announcement. Which kinds of information can `wc` report about the file? Choose all that apply.
options: How many lines are in the file
How many words are in the file
How many characters are in the file
The exact last sentence in the file
quizDraft.hint: Think about counts, not the actual text content.

help.concept: `wc` summarizes the amount of text in a file by reporting counts such as lines, words, and characters.
help.hint_1: Look for measurements of file content size rather than commands that display text.
help.hint_2: Three choices are count-based summaries that `wc` can provide.

quizDraft.title: Inspect a file in a sensible order
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange these actions in a sensible order for checking a text file in the terminal after entering a project folder.
quizDraft.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

quizDraft.title: Pick the right preview command
quizDraft.prompt: A teacher saved a long file named `chapter_notes.txt`, and you only want to inspect the beginning of it.
quizDraft.hint: Choose the command that shows the top of a file.

help.concept: `the missing term` shows the first part of a file, which is useful when you want a quick preview of how the file starts.
help.hint_1: This command is the opposite partner of `tail`.
help.hint_2: Use the command that matches the idea of the top or start of the file.

quizDraft.title: View a cafe menu file
quizDraft.prompt: You are helping a campus cafe manager check a short menu file. In the terminal, display the full contents of `menu.txt` so the terminal output shows every line of the file. This matters because `cat` is the fastest way to read a short text file all at once.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use the command that prints an entire file to the terminal.

help.concept: For short text files, `cat` displays the whole file directly in the terminal so you can inspect every line at once.
help.hint_1: You do not need to change folders or edit the file. Run one command on `menu.txt`.
help.hint_2: Use the command name from this topic that prints the complete contents of a file.

quizDraft.title: Create the park survey request folder
quizDraft.prompt: You are helping a park survey team prepare a terminal map. Create viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests and add viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt so the team has a concrete request log to hand off.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Use the terminal to make one small workspace change at a time, then inspect the result in the file explorer.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.starterCode: # Use the terminal for this Linux project task.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
mkdir -p viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests
printf 'North trail request log\n' > viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt
ls viewing-file-contents-park-survey
quizDraft.instructions: You are helping a park survey team prepare a terminal map. Create viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests and add viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt so the team has a concrete request log to hand off.

printf 'North trail request log\n' > viewing-file-contents-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt
